items.passage:
Mycorrhizae are symbiotic associations between certain fungi and the roots of most land plants, in which each partner supplies what the other lacks. The fungus extends thread-like filaments far beyond the reach of the roots, drawing scarce minerals such as phosphorus from the surrounding soil. In return, the plant transfers sugars produced through photosynthesis, feeding a partner that cannot manufacture its own food.

This exchange rests on the unusual structure of the fungal threads. Because they are far finer than roots, they penetrate tiny soil pores that root hairs cannot enter, sharply enlarging the volume of soil a plant can exploit. They also secrete enzymes that release nutrients locked in decaying matter, converting these elements into forms a plant can absorb.

The benefit, however, is mutual but not fixed. When soil is already rich in minerals, a plant may invest fewer sugars in its fungal partners, and the relationship can even tilt toward exploitation, as some plants draw resources from the shared network while giving little in return. Such imbalances suggest the partnership is better understood as a shifting negotiation than a stable contract.
paragraphs: Mycorrhizae are symbiotic associations between certain fungi and the roots of most land plants, in which each partner supplies what the other lacks. The fungus extends thread-like filaments far beyond the reach of the roots, drawing scarce minerals such as phosphorus from the surrounding soil. In return, the plant transfers sugars produced through photosynthesis, feeding a partner that cannot manufacture its own food.

This exchange rests on the unusual structure of the fungal threads. Because they are far finer than roots, they penetrate tiny soil pores that root hairs cannot enter, sharply enlarging the volume of soil a plant can exploit. They also secrete enzymes that release nutrients locked in decaying matter, converting these elements into forms a plant can absorb.

The benefit, however, is mutual but not fixed. When soil is already rich in minerals, a plant may invest fewer sugars in its fungal partners, and the relationship can even tilt toward exploitation, as some plants draw resources from the shared network while giving little in return. Such imbalances suggest the partnership is better understood as a shifting negotiation than a stable contract.

questions.stem: The word "scarce" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: hidden
options.B: harmful
options.C: heavy
options.D: limited

questions.explanation: The fungus draws "scarce minerals such as phosphorus" from the soil, so "scarce" means in short supply, that is, limited.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what does the plant provide to the fungus?

options.A: Sugars made through photosynthesis
options.B: Minerals such as phosphorus
options.C: Thread-like filaments for support
options.D: Enzymes that break down soil

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that "the plant transfers sugars produced through photosynthesis" to the fungus.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why can the fungal threads reach nutrients that roots cannot?

options.A: They grow much more quickly than roots do in cold soil.
options.B: They are finer than roots and enter tiny soil pores.
options.C: They store the water that slowly dissolves buried minerals.
options.D: They replace the root hairs that a plant gradually loses.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that "because they are far finer than roots, they penetrate tiny soil pores that root hairs cannot enter."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about a plant growing in mineral-rich soil?

options.A: It stops producing any sugars through photosynthesis.
options.B: It comes to depend more heavily on its fungal partners.
options.C: It has less to gain from the fungal partnership.
options.D: It becomes entirely unable to form mycorrhizae.

questions.explanation: Since a plant in rich soil "may invest fewer sugars in its fungal partners," it can be inferred that it benefits less from the partnership when minerals are abundant.

questions.stem: What does the author suggest by calling the partnership "a shifting negotiation than a stable contract" in paragraph 3?

options.A: The exchange always favors the fungus over the plant.
options.B: The two partners rarely exchange any resources at all.
options.C: The relationship ends once soil minerals are exhausted.
options.D: The balance of benefit between the partners can change.

questions.explanation: The phrase follows examples of the benefit being "mutual but not fixed," implying that the balance between partners varies rather than staying constant.

items.passage:
Echolocation is a biological sonar system by which certain animals perceive their surroundings using reflected sound rather than light. An animal emits a burst of high-frequency clicks and then listens for the echoes that bounce back from nearby objects. From the timing and direction of these returning echoes, it constructs a detailed picture of its environment in complete darkness.

This ability depends on remarkably precise control of both sound and hearing. A hunting bat, for instance, adjusts the pitch and spacing of its calls as it closes on prey, producing rapid bursts that sharpen its sense of an insect's exact position. Its ears, meanwhile, register differences in arrival time between them so small that they reveal whether a target lies slightly to the left or right.

Such acuity, however, carries a cost. Producing intense calls demands considerable energy, and the loud signals can be detected by the very prey a predator pursues. Some moths have evolved to hear approaching bats and take evasive action, suggesting that echolocation, for all its power, has driven an ongoing contest between hunter and hunted.

An animal emits a burst of high-frequency clicks and then listens for the echoes that bounce back from nearby objects. From the timing and direction of these returning echoes, it constructs a detailed picture of its environment in complete darkness.

This ability depends on remarkably precise control of both sound and hearing. A hunting bat, for instance, adjusts the pitch and spacing of its calls as it closes on prey, producing rapid bursts that sharpen its sense of an insect's exact position. Its ears, meanwhile, register differences in arrival time between them so small that they reveal whether a target lies slightly to the left or right.

Such acuity, however, carries a cost. Producing intense calls demands considerable energy, and the loud signals can be detected by the very prey a predator pursues. Some moths have evolved to hear approaching bats and take evasive action, suggesting that echolocation, for all its power, has driven an ongoing contest between hunter and hunted.

questions.stem: The word "emits" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: hears
options.B: records
options.C: absorbs
options.D: produces

questions.explanation: The animal "emits a burst of high-frequency clicks" and then listens for echoes, so "emits" means produces or sends out.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does a hunting bat change its calls as it nears prey?

options.A: It lowers their volume to avoid being detected.
options.B: It stops calling and instead relies on vision.
options.C: It varies their pitch and spacing into rapid bursts.
options.D: It produces a single long and steady tone.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that a bat "adjusts the pitch and spacing of its calls as it closes on prey, producing rapid bursts."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does a bat determine whether a target is to the left or right?

options.A: By the brightness of the reflected echo
options.B: By tiny differences in when sound reaches each ear
options.C: By the total number of clicks it produces
options.D: By the color of the object ahead of it

questions.explanation: The passage notes that the bat's ears "register differences in arrival time between them" precise enough to reveal whether a target lies left or right.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about moths that hear approaching bats?

options.A: They are more likely to survive a bat's attack.
options.B: They produce echolocation calls of their own.
options.C: They are unaffected by a bat's loud signals.
options.D: They attract bats by responding to the clicks.

questions.explanation: Because such moths "take evasive action" when they hear a bat, it can be inferred that this ability improves their chances of escaping.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention moths in paragraph 3?

options.A: To show that echolocation has provoked counter-adaptations in prey
options.B: To prove that bats cannot detect moving insects
options.C: To explain how moths produce their own sonar
options.D: To compare the hearing of moths and bats

questions.explanation: The moth example follows the point about the "cost" of loud calls and supports the idea of "an ongoing contest between hunter and hunted," illustrating prey adaptation.

items.passage:
Seed dormancy is a state in which a viable seed delays germination even when moisture, warmth, and oxygen are present. In its initial condition, a freshly shed seed is often locked shut by a hard coat or by chemical inhibitors that suppress growth. This built-in delay prevents the seed from sprouting at the wrong moment, such as just before a killing frost or a dry season.

This protective pause is gradually overcome through specific environmental signals. Many seeds require a prolonged period of cold and damp, which slowly breaks down the inhibiting compounds, while others must pass through fire or be abraded before water can penetrate the coat. Only after these conditions are met does the embryo resume the growth that dormancy had held in check.

The breaking of dormancy, however, does not guarantee survival. A seed that germinates after a single warm spell may perish if harsher weather returns, so the timing of release is itself subject to natural selection. Populations whose dormancy is finely tuned to local climate tend to leave more descendants, though shifting conditions can render once-reliable cues misleading.
paragraphs: Seed dormancy is a state in which a viable seed delays germination even when moisture, warmth, and oxygen are present. In its initial condition, a freshly shed seed is often locked shut by a hard coat or by chemical inhibitors that suppress growth. This built-in delay prevents the seed from sprouting at the wrong moment, such as just before a killing frost or a dry season.

Only after these conditions are met does the embryo resume the growth that dormancy had held in check.

The breaking of dormancy, however, does not guarantee survival. A seed that germinates after a single warm spell may perish if harsher weather returns, so the timing of release is itself subject to natural selection. Populations whose dormancy is finely tuned to local climate tend to leave more descendants, though shifting conditions can render once-reliable cues misleading.

questions.stem: The word "suppress" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: hold back
options.B: speed up
options.C: reveal
options.D: replace

questions.explanation: The chemical inhibitors "suppress growth," preventing the seed from sprouting, so "suppress" means hold back or restrain.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what is the purpose of a seed's built-in delay?

options.A: To allow the seed to grow a much thicker coat
options.B: To increase the total number of seeds a plant makes
options.C: To help the seed spread farther from its parent plant
options.D: To stop germination from beginning at a dangerous time

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the delay "prevents the seed from sprouting at the wrong moment, such as just before a killing frost or a dry season."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what allows some seeds to absorb water?

options.A: A long period of cold and damp weather
options.B: The presence of chemical inhibitors
options.C: Passing through fire or being abraded
options.D: Warmth combined with abundant oxygen

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that some seeds "must pass through fire or be abraded before water can penetrate the coat."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about seeds that need a long cold, damp period?

options.A: They will typically germinate right away once they are shed.
options.B: They are unlikely to sprout after only a brief warm spell.
options.C: They cannot germinate at all unless they are first burned.
options.D: They contain no growth-inhibiting chemicals of any kind.

questions.explanation: Since these seeds require "a prolonged period of cold and damp" before growth resumes, it follows that a short warm spell alone would not trigger them.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about shifting climate conditions?

options.A: They make dormancy entirely unnecessary.
options.B: They speed up the breakdown of seed coats.
options.C: They can make a population's timing cues unreliable.
options.D: They guarantee that more seeds will survive.

questions.explanation: The passage notes that "shifting conditions can render once-reliable cues misleading," implying that climate change can undermine well-tuned dormancy timing.

items.passage:
An earthquake is a sudden release of energy stored in the Earth's crust, producing seismic waves that travel outward from a point of origin. This energy accumulates slowly as tectonic plates press against one another along faults, regions where great blocks of rock meet. [■] When the stress finally exceeds the strength of the rock, the fault ruptures and the stored energy is discharged in seconds.

The location of this rupture shapes how an earthquake is felt at the surface. The point underground where slip begins is called the focus, while the spot directly above it is the epicenter. [■] Energy radiates from the focus as two principal wave types: faster compressional waves that arrive first, and slower shear waves that follow and typically cause greater shaking. [■] The interval between their arrivals allows analysts to estimate the distance to the source.

Measuring an earthquake's size, however, remains complicated. Magnitude scales express the energy released, but the damage felt at any location also depends on depth, soil composition, and building design. [■] Consequently, two earthquakes of identical magnitude may produce strikingly different outcomes, and forecasts of harm remain inherently uncertain.
paragraphs: An earthquake is a sudden release of energy stored in the Earth's crust, producing seismic waves that travel outward from a point of origin. This energy accumulates slowly as tectonic plates press against one another along faults, regions where great blocks of rock meet. [■] When the stress finally exceeds the strength of the rock, the fault ruptures and the stored energy is discharged in seconds.

The location of this rupture shapes how an earthquake is felt at the surface. The point underground where slip begins is called the focus, while the spot directly above it is the epicenter. [■] Energy radiates from the focus as two principal wave types: faster compressional waves that arrive first, and slower shear waves that follow and typically cause greater shaking. [■] The interval between their arrivals allows analysts to estimate the distance to the source.

Measuring an earthquake's size, however, remains complicated. Magnitude scales express the energy released, but the damage felt at any location also depends on depth, soil composition, and building design. [■] Consequently, two earthquakes of identical magnitude may produce strikingly different outcomes, and forecasts of harm remain inherently uncertain.

questions.stem: The word "discharged" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: absorbed
options.B: measured
options.C: weakened
options.D: released

questions.explanation: The stored energy is "discharged in seconds" when the fault ruptures, echoing the definition of an earthquake as a "release of energy," so "discharged" means released.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what is the focus of an earthquake?

options.A: The surface point directly above the rupture
options.B: The boundary between two tectonic plates
options.C: The underground point where slip begins
options.D: The region of greatest surface damage

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 defines the focus as "the point underground where slip begins," distinct from the epicenter above it.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following affect the damage an earthquake causes EXCEPT

options.A: the composition of the soil
options.B: the speed of the compressional waves
options.C: the depth at which the rupture occurs
options.D: the design of the affected buildings

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 lists depth, soil composition, and building design as factors in damage; wave speed is mentioned only as a way to estimate distance, not as a cause of damage.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about how analysts locate an earthquake's source?

options.A: They depend mainly on the measured height of the nearby buildings.
options.B: They calculate the total amount of energy the fault releases.
options.C: They use the time gap between the two wave types.
options.D: They directly observe the buried focus from the surface above.

questions.explanation: Because "the interval between their arrivals allows analysts to estimate the distance to the source," the timing gap between wave types is used to locate it.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a section of a plate boundary may remain locked for centuries, quietly building strain until it suddenly gives way.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after the sentence on plates pressing along faults)
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 2, after the sentence that defines focus and epicenter)
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, after the sentence describing the wave types)
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 3, after the sentence listing the damage factors)

questions.explanation: The inserted sentence elaborates on stress that "accumulates slowly as tectonic plates press against one another along faults," so it fits at slot 1, just before the rupture is described.

items.passage:
Superconductivity is a state in which certain materials conduct electric current with no resistance once cooled below a sharply defined critical temperature. In an ordinary conductor, electrons collide with vibrating atoms and lose energy as heat, which is why power lines waste part of what they carry. In a superconductor, by contrast, these collisions cease entirely, and a current set in motion can persist almost indefinitely.

This vanishing of resistance arises from a subtle change in how electrons behave. Below the critical temperature, electrons pair up and move through the material in a coordinated fashion, no longer scattering off the atomic lattice as isolated particles. Because the pairs travel in concert, the obstacles that normally impede single electrons no longer drain their energy.

The practical promise of superconductivity, however, is constrained by temperature. The critical points of most materials lie far below the cold of the Arctic, so maintaining them demands costly and cumbersome cooling. Although newer compounds superconduct at higher temperatures than once seemed possible, these still lie well below everyday conditions, and a reliable room-temperature superconductor remains beyond reach.
paragraphs: Superconductivity is a state in which certain materials conduct electric current with no resistance once cooled below a sharply defined critical temperature. In an ordinary conductor, electrons collide with vibrating atoms and lose energy as heat, which is why power lines waste part of what they carry. In a superconductor, by contrast, these collisions cease entirely, and a current set in motion can persist almost indefinitely.

This vanishing of resistance arises from a subtle change in how electrons behave. Below the critical temperature, electrons pair up and move through the material in a coordinated fashion, no longer scattering off the atomic lattice as isolated particles. Because the pairs travel in concert, the obstacles that normally impede single electrons no longer drain their energy.

The practical promise of superconductivity, however, is constrained by temperature. The critical points of most materials lie far below the cold of the Arctic, so maintaining them demands costly and cumbersome cooling. Although newer compounds superconduct at higher temperatures than once seemed possible, these still lie well below everyday conditions, and a reliable room-temperature superconductor remains beyond reach.

questions.stem: The word "cease" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: stop
options.B: slow
options.C: return
options.D: spread

questions.explanation: In a superconductor the electron collisions "cease entirely," allowing current to persist, so "cease" means stop.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why do ordinary power lines waste energy?

options.A: Their electrons pair up and travel through the wire together.
options.B: They are never cooled below their own critical temperature.
options.C: Their electrons strike vibrating atoms and shed energy as heat.
options.D: They can carry current in only one fixed direction.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that in an ordinary conductor electrons "collide with vibrating atoms and lose energy as heat."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about single, unpaired electrons in a material?

options.A: They move through the material without losing any energy.
options.B: They scatter off the lattice and so lose energy.
options.C: They are wholly unable to carry electric current.
options.D: They appear only above the critical temperature.

questions.explanation: Since paired electrons "no longer scattering off the atomic lattice" avoid energy loss, it can be inferred that isolated electrons do scatter and lose energy.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention "the cold of the Arctic" in paragraph 3?

options.A: To suggest that superconductors work best in polar regions
options.B: To explain how the cooling equipment is manufactured
options.C: To compare two rival superconducting compounds in use
options.D: To convey how extremely low the required temperatures are

questions.explanation: The comparison stresses that critical temperatures "lie far below the cold of the Arctic," emphasizing how difficult the necessary cooling is.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about a room-temperature superconductor?

options.A: It has already begun to replace ordinary power lines.
options.B: It would remove a major barrier to practical use.
options.C: It conducts electric current less efficiently than cold ones.
options.D: It would require even colder conditions than older materials.

questions.explanation: Because the chief obstacle is the "costly and cumbersome cooling" required, a superconductor working at room temperature would overcome that limitation.

items.passage:
Water treatment is the process of removing contaminants from raw water to make it safe for human use. Although the specific contaminants vary by source, most municipal systems rely on a sequence of physical and chemical stages, each targeting a different class of impurity. The aim is not absolute purity but water that meets defined safety standards reliably and at an acceptable cost.

This sequence begins with stages that address the particles suspended in the water. In coagulation, chemicals are added that cause fine particles to clump into larger masses called floc, which then settle to the bottom of a tank during sedimentation. Filtration follows, as the clarified water passes through layers of sand and gravel that trap the remaining solids and the cloudiness sheltering harmful organisms.

These physical stages still leave behind microorganisms that they cannot fully eliminate. The final stage, disinfection, usually with chlorine or ultraviolet light, destroys the bacteria and viruses that survive filtration. Chlorine offers a lasting benefit, since a small residual amount remains in the pipes, guarding against later contamination. However, disinfection can generate unwanted chemical byproducts, so operators must balance safety against these unintended effects.
paragraphs: Water treatment is the process of removing contaminants from raw water to make it safe for human use. Although the specific contaminants vary by source, most municipal systems rely on a sequence of physical and chemical stages, each targeting a different class of impurity. The aim is not absolute purity but water that meets defined safety standards reliably and at an acceptable cost.

This sequence begins with stages that address the particles suspended in the water. In coagulation, chemicals are added that cause fine particles to clump into larger masses called floc, which then settle to the bottom of a tank during sedimentation. Filtration follows, as the clarified water passes through layers of sand and gravel that trap the remaining solids and the cloudiness sheltering harmful organisms.

These physical stages still leave behind microorganisms that they cannot fully eliminate. The final stage, disinfection, usually with chlorine or ultraviolet light, destroys the bacteria and viruses that survive filtration. Chlorine offers a lasting benefit, since a small residual amount remains in the pipes, guarding against later contamination. However, disinfection can generate unwanted chemical byproducts, so operators must balance safety against these unintended effects.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The reasons many cities prefer chlorine to ultraviolet light
options.B: The health risks created by drinking contaminated tap water
options.C: The history of how early municipal water systems were built
options.D: The sequence of stages used to make raw water safe

questions.explanation: The passage defines water treatment and then walks through its successive physical and chemical stages, so it is mainly about the stages that make water safe.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens during coagulation?

options.A: Water is passed through deep layers of sand and gravel.
options.B: Chlorine is added to destroy the bacteria and viruses.
options.C: Chemicals make fine particles gather into larger clumps.
options.D: A residual chemical is deliberately left in the pipes.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that in coagulation "chemicals are added that cause fine particles to clump into larger masses called floc."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It presents a later stage that handles what earlier ones cannot.
options.B: It dismisses the methods of paragraph 2 as outdated and ineffective.
options.C: It repeats the coagulation process using a different set of chemicals.
options.D: It contrasts two competing theories about how the particles settle.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 describes "the final stage" of disinfection, which destroys the microorganisms that the physical stages of paragraph 2 "still leave behind."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about using chlorine for disinfection?

options.A: It kills bacteria far less effectively than ultraviolet light does.
options.B: It removes suspended particles better than ordinary filtration.
options.C: Its benefits must be weighed against the byproducts it creates.
options.D: It does away with the need for any earlier treatment stage.

questions.explanation: Because disinfection "can generate unwanted chemical byproducts" that operators must "balance" against safety, chlorine's benefits come with trade-offs.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what advantage of chlorine extends beyond the treatment plant?

options.A: It acts much faster than ultraviolet light during disinfection.
options.B: A residual amount stays in the pipes to block later contamination.
options.C: It strips out the byproducts created by the earlier stages.
options.D: It lets operators skip the filtration step altogether.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that with chlorine "a small residual amount remains in the pipes, guarding against later contamination."

items.passage:
A trade network is a set of interconnected routes and relationships through which goods, people, and information move between distant communities. Such networks form the backbone of commercial exchange, linking producers in one region to consumers in another and allowing specialized goods to circulate widely. [■] Their reach depends not only on geography but also on the institutions that govern how exchange is conducted.

Within these networks, intermediaries play a decisive role. Merchants stationed at key junctions buy goods from incoming traders and resell them onward, absorbing the risks of transport and price swings. [■] By gathering trade at fixed points, such intermediaries lower the cost of finding trading partners and checking the quality of goods. [■] This efficiency helps explain why certain ports and market towns grew quickly into busy commercial hubs.

Beyond these mechanics, the stability of any trade network ultimately rests on trust. Because participants are often strangers separated by great distances, mechanisms such as reputation, written contracts, and shared legal customs become essential. [■] However, these arrangements remain fragile, and a single episode of fraud or political disruption can sever ties that took generations to establish.
paragraphs: A trade network is a set of interconnected routes and relationships through which goods, people, and information move between distant communities. Such networks form the backbone of commercial exchange, linking producers in one region to consumers in another and allowing specialized goods to circulate widely. [■] Their reach depends not only on geography but also on the institutions that govern how exchange is conducted.

Within these networks, intermediaries play a decisive role. Merchants stationed at key junctions buy goods from incoming traders and resell them onward, absorbing the risks of transport and price swings. [■] By gathering trade at fixed points, such intermediaries lower the cost of finding trading partners and checking the quality of goods. [■] This efficiency helps explain why certain ports and market towns grew quickly into busy commercial hubs.

Beyond these mechanics, the stability of any trade network ultimately rests on trust. Because participants are often strangers separated by great distances, mechanisms such as reputation, written contracts, and shared legal customs become essential. [■] However, these arrangements remain fragile, and a single episode of fraud or political disruption can sever ties that took generations to establish.

questions.stem: The word "absorbing" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: studying
options.B: soaking
options.C: bearing
options.D: reducing

questions.explanation: The merchants take on "the risks of transport and price swings" themselves, so "absorbing" means bearing or assuming those risks.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how do intermediaries lower costs within a trade network?

options.A: They concentrate exchange at fixed sites, easing the assessment of partners and goods.
options.B: They personally carry the goods along the longest and most dangerous of routes.
options.C: They impose uniform prices that all of the incoming traders are obliged to accept.
options.D: They eliminate any need for written contracts between the most distant traders.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that "by gathering trade at fixed points, such intermediaries lower the cost of finding trading partners and checking the quality of goods."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about trust in trade networks?

options.A: It matters steadily less as a network grows larger and richer.
options.B: It is fully guaranteed once written contracts have been introduced.
options.C: It forms automatically among any traders who share one language.
options.D: It is difficult to rebuild quickly once it has been broken.

questions.explanation: Since a single episode of fraud can "sever ties that took generations to establish," it follows that broken trust cannot be quickly restored.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention "ports and market towns" in paragraph 2?

options.A: To illustrate a result of the efficiency that intermediaries create
options.B: To contrast distant coastal trading sites with inland ones
options.C: To argue that geography matters far more than institutions
options.D: To introduce a specific and dramatic historical case of fraud

questions.explanation: The towns are cited as an outcome: "This efficiency helps explain why certain ports and market towns grew quickly into busy commercial hubs."

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a spice dealer in a busy harbor might buy an entire shipload at once and pass it to dozens of smaller buyers over the following weeks.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (at the end of the first paragraph)
options.B: Slot 2 (after the sentence about merchants reselling goods onward)
options.C: Slot 3 (after the sentence about lowering trading costs)
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 3, after the sentence about trust mechanisms)

questions.explanation: The inserted sentence is a concrete example of merchants who "buy goods from incoming traders and resell them onward," so it fits directly after that sentence at slot 2.

items.passage:
The Industrial Revolution was a period of rapid technological and economic change that transformed manufacturing from handwork into machine-based production. In its earliest stage, goods were made at home under the putting-out system, in which merchants supplied raw materials to rural families who spun and wove by hand. [■] Output was limited, since each worker could operate only one simple device at a time.

This household model gave way to the factory as new machines demanded concentrated power. Early factories were built beside fast-flowing rivers, whose water wheels drove rows of mechanical looms. [■] Yet rivers froze in winter and ran dry in summer, and their fixed locations forced workers to migrate to remote valleys. [■] These limitations restricted where production could occur and how steadily it could continue.

The steam engine resolved these constraints and marked the breakthrough stage. Because it burned coal rather than relying on flowing water, a steam-powered mill could be built almost anywhere, including inside growing cities. [■] This freedom concentrated factories in urban centers, drawing vast populations into towns whose rapid, unplanned growth would strain housing and sanitation for decades.
paragraphs: The Industrial Revolution was a period of rapid technological and economic change that transformed manufacturing from handwork into machine-based production. In its earliest stage, goods were made at home under the putting-out system, in which merchants supplied raw materials to rural families who spun and wove by hand. [■] Output was limited, since each worker could operate only one simple device at a time.

This household model gave way to the factory as new machines demanded concentrated power. Early factories were built beside fast-flowing rivers, whose water wheels drove rows of mechanical looms. [■] Yet rivers froze in winter and ran dry in summer, and their fixed locations forced workers to migrate to remote valleys. [■] These limitations restricted where production could occur and how steadily it could continue.

The steam engine resolved these constraints and marked the breakthrough stage. Because it burned coal rather than relying on flowing water, a steam-powered mill could be built almost anywhere, including inside growing cities. [■] This freedom concentrated factories in urban centers, drawing vast populations into towns whose rapid, unplanned growth would strain housing and sanitation for decades.

questions.stem: The word "constraints" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: expenses
options.B: engines
options.C: benefits
options.D: limitations

questions.explanation: The steam engine "resolved these constraints," referring to the river-power "limitations" of paragraph 2, so "constraints" means limitations.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how did the putting-out system work?

options.A: Workers gathered together in factories to run the powered machines.
options.B: Merchants sold the finished cloth directly back to the rural families.
options.C: Merchants handed out raw fiber to rural households weaving at home.
options.D: Families bought their own materials and sold finished goods in cities.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that under the putting-out system "merchants supplied raw materials to rural families who spun and wove by hand."

questions.stem: What does "their" refer to in the phrase "their fixed locations" in paragraph 2?

options.A: rivers
options.B: water wheels
options.C: mechanical looms
options.D: workers

questions.explanation: The sentence says "rivers froze in winter and ran dry in summer, and their fixed locations forced workers to migrate," so "their" refers to the rivers.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about steam-powered mills?

options.A: They turned out far less cloth than the older water-powered factories.
options.B: They let owners pick sites by labor and markets rather than water.
options.C: They were still usually built in remote valleys far away from cities.
options.D: They depended on flowing rivers even more than earlier factories had.

questions.explanation: Since a steam mill "could be built almost anywhere, including inside growing cities," it can be inferred that owners were no longer tied to water sources.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For instance, a single spinner working a hand-operated wheel might take an entire day to produce just a few skeins of thread.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after the sentence about operating one simple device)
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 2, after the sentence about water wheels driving looms)
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, after the sentence about rivers freezing)
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 3, after the sentence about building mills anywhere)

questions.explanation: The inserted sentence gives a concrete instance of limited output when "each worker could operate only one simple device at a time," so it fits at slot 1.

items.passage:
A central bank is a public institution that manages a nation's currency and oversees the supply of money available to its economy. Unlike commercial banks, which serve individual customers, a central bank serves the banking system as a whole, acting as banker to other banks and to the government. Its decisions shape the cost of borrowing throughout the economy.

Its most familiar tool is the adjustment of a key interest rate. By raising the rate at which commercial banks borrow, the central bank makes credit more expensive, discouraging spending and cooling an overheating economy; by lowering it, the bank encourages borrowing and stimulates activity. These adjustments ripple outward to the rates that households and firms eventually pay.

Beyond setting interest rates, a central bank also acts as a lender of last resort during financial panics. When fear drives depositors to withdraw funds at once, the bank can supply emergency cash to solvent institutions, preventing a single failure from spreading. However, such rescues may encourage reckless behavior, since banks that expect to be saved have less reason to manage risk prudently.
paragraphs: A central bank is a public institution that manages a nation's currency and oversees the supply of money available to its economy. Unlike commercial banks, which serve individual customers, a central bank serves the banking system as a whole, acting as banker to other banks and to the government. Its decisions shape the cost of borrowing throughout the economy.

Its most familiar tool is the adjustment of a key interest rate. By raising the rate at which commercial banks borrow, the central bank makes credit more expensive, discouraging spending and cooling an overheating economy; by lowering it, the bank encourages borrowing and stimulates activity. These adjustments ripple outward to the rates that households and firms eventually pay.

Beyond setting interest rates, a central bank also acts as a lender of last resort during financial panics. When fear drives depositors to withdraw funds at once, the bank can supply emergency cash to solvent institutions, preventing a single failure from spreading. However, such rescues may encourage reckless behavior, since banks that expect to be saved have less reason to manage risk prudently.

questions.stem: The word "oversees" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: supplies
options.B: borrows
options.C: ignores
options.D: supervises

questions.explanation: A central bank "oversees the supply of money" available to the economy, so "oversees" means supervises or manages.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens when a central bank raises its key interest rate?

options.A: Credit becomes cheaper and spending rises.
options.B: Credit becomes more expensive and spending falls.
options.C: Commercial banks stop lending to the government.
options.D: Households are required to deposit more money.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that raising the rate "makes credit more expensive, discouraging spending and cooling an overheating economy."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what does a central bank do as a lender of last resort?

options.A: It provides urgent funds to still-sound banks during a panic.
options.B: It lowers the key interest rate to zero for every bank.
options.C: It takes over the running of any failing commercial bank.
options.D: It guarantees the deposits held by each individual customer.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 explains that during a panic "the bank can supply emergency cash to solvent institutions, preventing a single failure from spreading."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about banks that expect to be rescued?

options.A: They are more careful about the risks they take.
options.B: They tend to lend less money to households.
options.C: They may take greater risks than they otherwise would.
options.D: They no longer rely on the central bank for cash.

questions.explanation: Because such rescues "may encourage reckless behavior," since rescued banks "have less reason to manage risk prudently," it follows that they may take on more risk.

questions.stem: Why does the author contrast central banks with commercial banks in paragraph 1?

options.A: To show that the institution serves the whole system, not individuals
options.B: To argue that commercial banks are now no longer necessary at all
options.C: To explain how ordinary individuals open accounts at a central bank
options.D: To prove that central banks earn much larger profits than others

questions.explanation: The contrast clarifies the central bank's distinctive role: "a central bank serves the banking system as a whole," not individual customers.

items.passage:
Cave art refers to the painted and engraved images created on rock surfaces deep inside caves, primarily during the last Ice Age. The earliest known examples are simple in form: hand stencils and rows of dots, made by blowing pigment around a hand or pressing colored fingers against the wall. These marks required little technical skill, but they reveal a deliberate intent to leave a lasting trace.

Later compositions reveal a more sophisticated stage of development. Artists began to depict animals such as bison and horses with shading and overlapping outlines that suggest movement and depth. To achieve such effects, they ground minerals into pigments, raised scaffolds to reach high walls, and exploited the natural contours of the rock. This growing mastery indicates that image-making had become a practiced and transmitted craft.

Interpreting the purpose of these images, however, remains contested. Because the painted chambers were often remote and dark, scholars doubt that the art was meant for ordinary viewing. Some propose that the images served ritual or instructional functions, though without written records any such explanation must remain provisional.
paragraphs: Cave art refers to the painted and engraved images created on rock surfaces deep inside caves, primarily during the last Ice Age. The earliest known examples are simple in form: hand stencils and rows of dots, made by blowing pigment around a hand or pressing colored fingers against the wall. These marks required little technical skill, but they reveal a deliberate intent to leave a lasting trace.

Later compositions reveal a more sophisticated stage of development. Artists began to depict animals such as bison and horses with shading and overlapping outlines that suggest movement and depth. To achieve such effects, they ground minerals into pigments, raised scaffolds to reach high walls, and exploited the natural contours of the rock. This growing mastery indicates that image-making had become a practiced and transmitted craft.

Interpreting the purpose of these images, however, remains contested. Because the painted chambers were often remote and dark, scholars doubt that the art was meant for ordinary viewing. Some propose that the images served ritual or instructional functions, though without written records any such explanation must remain provisional.

questions.stem: The word "exploited" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: concealed
options.B: flattened
options.C: used
options.D: damaged

questions.explanation: The artists "exploited the natural contours of the rock" to create depth, meaning they made use of those contours, so "exploited" is closest to used.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did artists do to suggest movement and depth?

options.A: They limited each painted image to a single color.
options.B: They avoided the high or curved parts of the cave wall.
options.C: They shaded the animals and let their outlines overlap.
options.D: They copied the earliest stencils onto fresh rock surfaces.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that artists depicted animals "with shading and overlapping outlines that suggest movement and depth."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about cave painting over time?

options.A: It grew steadily simpler as the mineral pigments became scarce.
options.B: It was practiced only by isolated and largely untrained individuals.
options.C: It remained wholly unchanged from the earliest hand stencils.
options.D: It was taught and passed from one group to another.

questions.explanation: The claim that image-making "had become a practiced and transmitted craft" implies techniques were taught and handed down rather than reinvented.

questions.stem: Why does the author note that the chambers were "remote and dark" in paragraph 3?

options.A: To explain why so few of the cave paintings have survived
options.B: To support the view that the images were not for ordinary viewing
options.C: To argue that the paintings were the work of trained specialists
options.D: To contrast Ice Age caves with the later open-air art

questions.explanation: The remoteness and darkness are given as the reason "scholars doubt that the art was meant for ordinary viewing," supporting that interpretation.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of the earliest cave art EXCEPT

options.A: It used shading to suggest depth in animals.
options.B: It included hand stencils and rows of dots.
options.C: It required little technical skill to make.
options.D: It showed an intent to leave a lasting trace.

questions.explanation: Shading and depth belong to the "later compositions" of paragraph 2; the earliest art in paragraph 1 was simple stencils and dots requiring little skill.

items.passage:
The printing press is a mechanical device that transfers inked, raised letters onto paper to reproduce text in quantity. Before its spread in Europe, books were copied by hand, a slow labor that confined written knowledge to the wealthy and the clergy. A single manuscript could take a scribe months to finish, and errors multiplied with every fresh copy.

This laborious system was transformed by the introduction of movable type. Individual metal letters could be arranged into a page, inked, and pressed onto sheet after sheet, then rearranged for the next page entirely. Because the same letters served countless texts, a printer could produce in a day what a scribe took a year to complete, and identical copies now emerged free of the drift that plagued hand-copying.

The consequences of cheaper books, however, reached far beyond the workshop. As printed texts spread, literacy widened and new ideas circulated faster than any authority could easily control. Rulers and churches that had once restricted knowledge found printed pages slipping past their oversight, a loss of command whose effects would unsettle established order for generations.
paragraphs: The printing press is a mechanical device that transfers inked, raised letters onto paper to reproduce text in quantity. Before its spread in Europe, books were copied by hand, a slow labor that confined written knowledge to the wealthy and the clergy. A single manuscript could take a scribe months to finish, and errors multiplied with every fresh copy.

This laborious system was transformed by the introduction of movable type. Individual metal letters could be arranged into a page, inked, and pressed onto sheet after sheet, then rearranged for the next page entirely. Because the same letters served countless texts, a printer could produce in a day what a scribe took a year to complete, and identical copies now emerged free of the drift that plagued hand-copying.

The consequences of cheaper books, however, reached far beyond the workshop. As printed texts spread, literacy widened and new ideas circulated faster than any authority could easily control. Rulers and churches that had once restricted knowledge found printed pages slipping past their oversight, a loss of command whose effects would unsettle established order for generations.

questions.stem: The word "confined" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: restricted
options.B: explained
options.C: donated
options.D: copied

questions.explanation: Hand-copying "confined written knowledge to the wealthy and the clergy," so "confined" means limited or restricted.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what was one drawback of copying books by hand?

options.A: The same letters could later be reused for many other texts.
options.B: Mistakes grew more numerous with each new copy made.
options.C: Books became far too cheap for the scribes to profit.
options.D: Pages were finished faster than readers could read them.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 notes that a manuscript "could take a scribe months to finish, and errors multiplied with every fresh copy."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why could movable type print so much faster than a scribe?

options.A: The metal letters were larger and easier to read.
options.B: Each page required an entirely new set of letters.
options.C: The same letters could be reused for countless texts.
options.D: Printers employed far more workers than scribes did.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that "because the same letters served countless texts, a printer could produce in a day what a scribe took a year to complete."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about authorities before the printing press?

options.A: They had actively encouraged widespread literacy among the public.
options.B: They had shown little interest in restricting access to knowledge.
options.C: They had printed many of their own texts for wide distribution.
options.D: They had found it easier to control what people read.

questions.explanation: Since printed pages began "slipping past their oversight," it can be inferred that authorities had previously found knowledge easier to control.

questions.stem: Why does the author describe hand-copying in paragraph 1?

options.A: To establish the slow, costly system that printing later replaced
options.B: To argue that the scribes actually produced more accurate books
options.C: To explain in detail how the movable type letters were cast
options.D: To show that printed books remained rare even after printing

questions.explanation: The paragraph details the laborious manuscript system as the baseline that "was transformed by the introduction of movable type," setting up the contrast.

items.passage:
A writing system is a set of visible marks used to record language in a form that can be read later. The earliest systems were pictographic, using small pictures in which a drawing of the sun stood for the sun and a drawing of an ox for an ox. Such signs were easy to grasp but clumsy, since abstract ideas and grammatical words resisted any simple picture.

This limitation drove a gradual shift toward signs that represented sounds rather than things. Scribes began to use a picture for its spoken value, so that a sign for a concrete object could stand for any word containing that sound. This principle let a manageable set of symbols express a vast range of meanings, including notions no picture could capture directly.

This principle was carried to its furthest point by the alphabet. By assigning one sign to each consonant or vowel, it reduced the symbols a reader must learn from thousands to a few dozen. Yet no writing system records speech perfectly, and every script leaves features of the spoken language, such as tone or stress, only partly represented.
paragraphs: A writing system is a set of visible marks used to record language in a form that can be read later. The earliest systems were pictographic, using small pictures in which a drawing of the sun stood for the sun and a drawing of an ox for an ox. Such signs were easy to grasp but clumsy, since abstract ideas and grammatical words resisted any simple picture.

This limitation drove a gradual shift toward signs that represented sounds rather than things. Scribes began to use a picture for its spoken value, so that a sign for a concrete object could stand for any word containing that sound. This principle let a manageable set of symbols express a vast range of meanings, including notions no picture could capture directly.

This principle was carried to its furthest point by the alphabet. By assigning one sign to each consonant or vowel, it reduced the symbols a reader must learn from thousands to a few dozen. Yet no writing system records speech perfectly, and every script leaves features of the spoken language, such as tone or stress, only partly represented.

questions.stem: The word "resisted" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: required
options.B: recorded
options.C: resembled
options.D: defied

questions.explanation: Abstract ideas "resisted any simple picture," meaning they could not easily be drawn, so "resisted" is closest to defied.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why were pictographic signs clumsy?

options.A: They could be read only by specially trained scribes.
options.B: Abstract ideas and grammar words defied any simple picture.
options.C: They required one separate sign for every single sound.
options.D: They were too small in size to be seen clearly.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that the signs were clumsy "since abstract ideas and grammatical words resisted any simple picture."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what new principle did scribes adopt?

options.A: Using a picture for the sound it named
options.B: Drawing each object in much greater detail
options.C: Assigning one sign to every whole word
options.D: Recording tone and stress signs directly

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that scribes "began to use a picture for its spoken value," so a sign could stand for any word containing that sound.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about the alphabet compared with earlier systems?

options.A: It records nearly every feature of speech with full precision.
options.B: It is far harder for an ordinary reader to memorize.
options.C: It requires a reader to learn far fewer signs.
options.D: It abandoned the use of sounds almost entirely.

questions.explanation: Because the alphabet "reduced the symbols a reader must learn from thousands to a few dozen," it requires learning far fewer signs than earlier systems.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about features such as tone or stress?

options.A: They are captured in full by ordinary alphabetic writing.
options.B: They make a writing system noticeably easier to read.
options.C: They appear only within older pictographic systems.
options.D: They are left only partly shown in written form.

questions.explanation: Since "every script leaves features of the spoken language, such as tone or stress, only partly represented," such features are not fully recorded in writing.
